name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ قُتِلُوٓا۟ أَوْ مَاتُوا۟ لَيَرْزُقَنَّهُمُ ٱللَّهُ رِزْقًا حَسَنًا ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ (٥٨)
```

```english
Those who migrate in the way of Allah and then are slain, or die, Allah will surely provide them with a good provision. Allah is indeed the best of providers. (58)
```

```arabic
لَيُدْخِلَنَّهُم مُّدْخَلًا يَرْضَوْنَهُۥ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلِيمٌ حَلِيمٌ (٥٩)
```

```english
He will admit them into an abode they are pleased with. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-forbearing. (59)
```

```arabic
۞ ذَٰلِكَ وَمَنْ عَاقَبَ بِمِثْلِ مَا عُوقِبَ بِهِۦ ثُمَّ بُغِىَ عَلَيْهِ لَيَنصُرَنَّهُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ (٦٠)
```

```english
So will it be; and whoever retaliates with the like of what he has been made to suffer, and is aggressed against [again] thereafter, Allah will surely help him. Indeed Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving. (60)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَيُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ (٦١)
```

```english
So will it be, because Allah makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night, and because Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing. (61)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَأَنَّ مَا يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ هُوَ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْكَبِيرُ (٦٢)
```

```english
So will it be, because Allah is the Reality, and what they invoke besides Him is nullity, and because Allah is the All-exalted, the All-great. (62)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَتُصْبِحُ ٱلْأَرْضُ مُخْضَرَّةً ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَطِيفٌ خَبِيرٌ (٦٣)
```

```english
Have you not regarded that Allah sends down water from the sky, whereupon the earth turns green? Indeed Allah is all-attentive, all-aware. (63)
```

```arabic
لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ (٦٤)
```

```english
To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, Indeed Allah is the All-sufficient, the All-laudable. (64)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَخَّرَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلْفُلْكَ تَجْرِى فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِۦ وَيُمْسِكُ ٱلسَّمَآءَ أَن تَقَعَ عَلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِٱلنَّاسِ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٦٥)
```

```english
Have you not regarded that Allah has disposed for you [r benefit] whatever there is in the earth, and [that] the ships sail at sea by His command, and He sustains the sky lest it should fall on the earth, excepting [when it does so] by His leave? Indeed Allah is most kind and merciful to mankind. (65)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَحْيَاكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَكَفُورٌ (٦٦)
```

```english
It is He who gave you life then He makes you die, then He brings you to life. Indeed man is very ungrateful. (66)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses follow the previous objective and clarify the reward of those who migrated and were subsequently killed while struggling in the way of Allah or died. They contain some incitement to fighting and a promise of victory, as indicated by His saying: **ذَلِكَ وَمَنْ عَاقَبَ بِمِثْلِ مَا عُوقِبَ بِهِ ثُمَّ بُغِيَ عَلَيْهِ لَيَنْصُرَنَّهُ اللَّهُ** "That [is so]; and whoever retaliates with the like of what he was made to suffer, and is then oppressed, Allah will surely help him" (22:60).

These verses possess a unique characteristic not found anywhere else in the entire Holy Quran except here: they are eight consecutive verses, each ending with two of the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) of Allah besides the Majestic Name (لفظ الجلالة) "Allah." Based on the view that the pronoun "He" (هو) functions as a name, sixteen names are gathered therein: "Allah is surely the best of providers" (خير الرازقين), "the All-Knowing" (العليم), "the All-Forbearing" (الحليم), "the All-Clement" (العفو), "the All-Forgiving" (الغفور), "the All-Hearing" (السميع), "the All-Seeing" (البصير), "the All-Exalted" (العلي), "the All-Great" (الكبير), "the Most Subtle" (اللطيف), "the All-Aware" (الخبير), "the All-Sufficient" (الغني), "the All-Laudable" (الحميد), "the Most Affectionate" (الرءوف), and "the Most Merciful" (الرحيم). Then, in the ninth verse, He mentions that the Exalted gives life and causes death, and within these verses, it is mentioned that He is the Truth (الحق) and that to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. These carry the meaning of four names: the Giver of Life (المحيي), the Bringer of Death (المميت), the Truth (الحق), and the Owner (المالك) or the King (الملك). Thus, twenty of His names are gathered in these eight verses in the most subtle and exquisite manner.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ ثُمَّ قُتِلُوا أَوْ مَاتُوا لَيَرْزُقَنَّهُمْ اللَّهُ رِزْقاً حَسَناً** "As for those who migrate in the way of Allah and then are slain or die, Allah will surely provide them with a good provision" (22:58). Since He previously mentioned the wrongful expulsion of the migrants from their homes, He follows it by mentioning the reward He will grant them for their migration and their ordeal in the way of Allah, which is a beautiful promise of a good provision.

He restricted migration (الهجرة) — *the act of leaving one's homeland for the sake of faith* — to being "in the way of Allah" because reward is only contingent upon righteous deeds (صالح العمل). A deed is only righteous in the sight of Allah through sincerity of intention (خلوص النية) and by being for His sake, not for the sake of others such as wealth, status, or other worldly objectives. Similarly, His saying "then are slain or die" is restricted by the same condition; meaning they were killed in the way of Allah or died while they were in exile for His sake.

His saying: **وَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُوَ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ** "and Allah is indeed the best of providers" (22:58) is a conclusion to the verse that provides the reason for the "good provision" mentioned therein. This refers to the blessings of the Hereafter, as its station is after being slain or dying. In this verse, "provision" (الرزق) is applied generally to the blessings of Paradise, as in His saying: **أَحْيَاءٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ** "they are living with their Lord, provided for" (3:169).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَيُدْخِلَنَّهُمْ مُدْخَلاً يَرْضَوْنَهُ وَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لَعَلِيمٌ حَلِيمٌ** "He will surely admit them to an entrance they will be pleased with, and Allah is indeed all-knowing, all-forbearing" (22:59). The word "entrance" (المدخل), with a *damma* on the *mim* and a *fatha* on the *kha*, is a noun of place (اسم مكان) derived from "admitting" (الإدخال). The possibility of it being a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) does not suit the context as well.

Describing this entrance—which is Paradise—with His saying "they will be pleased with" (يرضونه), where the pleasure (الرضا) is absolute, is evidence that it encompasses the utmost of what a human being desires, as He said: **لَهُمْ فِيهَا مَا يَشَاءُونَ** "They will have therein whatever they wish" (25:16).

His saying: "He will surely admit them to an entrance they will be pleased with" is an elaboration of His saying: "Allah will surely provide them with a good provision." His admitting them to an entrance they are pleased with and do not dislike is in contrast to the polytheists' (المشركين) expelling them in a manner they disliked and were not pleased with. Therefore, He justified it by saying: "and Allah is indeed all-knowing, all-forbearing"; meaning He is All-Knowing (عليم) of what pleases them and thus prepares it for them, and He is All-Forbearing (حليم) and does not hasten the punishment for their enemies who wronged them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذَلِكَ وَمَنْ عَاقَبَ بِمِثْلِ مَا عُوقِبَ بِهِ ثُمَّ بُغِيَ عَلَيْهِ لَيَنْصُرَنَّهُ اللَّهُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ** "That [is so]; and whoever retaliates with the like of what he was made to suffer, and is then oppressed, Allah will surely help him. Indeed Allah is all-clement, all-forgiving" (22:60). The word "That" (ذلك) is the predicate of a deleted subject, meaning: "The matter is that which We have informed you of and mentioned to you." Punishment (العقاب) is holding a person accountable with what they dislike in exchange for their committing what the punisher does not approve of; it is called *‘iqāb* because it comes "after" (*‘aqīb*) the act.

"Retaliating with the like of the punishment" is a metaphor for reciprocal treatment (المعاملة بالمثل). Since this reciprocal treatment is only good when the initial punishment was without right, He restricted it by its being "oppression" (بغياً), thus conjoining His saying "and is then oppressed" (بُغِيَ عَلَيْهِ) with the particle "then" (ثم).

His saying: "Allah will surely help him"—the apparent context, being a station of granting permission for struggle (الجهاد), suggests that the intended "help" (النصر) is the triumph of the oppressed over the oppressive transgressors and supporting them against them in battle. However, it can be inferred from verses like: **وَمَنْ قُتِلَ مَظْلُوماً فَقَدْ جَعَلْنَا لِوَلِيِّهِ سُلْطَاناً فَلا يُسْرِفْ فِي الْقَتْلِ إِنَّهُ كَانَ مَنْصُوراً** "and whoever is killed wrongfully, We have certainly given his heir authority. But let him not commit any excess in killing—indeed he has been helped" (17:33), that the intended "help" is the legislation of a ruling for the oppressed person by which the stain of injustice and oppression is redressed. For in His permission for the oppressed to treat the transgressor with the like of what he did, He has extended the hand of the one over whom a hand was previously extended.

Through this, the meaning of justifying the help with His saying: "Indeed Allah is all-clement, all-forgiving" becomes clear. Permission and lawfulness in cases of necessity (الاضطرار), hardship (الحرج), and the like are among the requirements of the attributes of clemency (العفو) and forgiveness (المغفرة), as has been mentioned repeatedly regarding verses such as: **فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ فِي مَخْمَصَةٍ غَيْرَ مُتَجَانِفٍ لإِثْمٍ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** "But should anyone be compelled by hunger, without inclining to sin, then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful" (5:3). We have clarified this regarding retribution and forgiveness at the end of the sixth volume of this book.

The meaning—based on this—is: whoever treats the one who punished him oppressively with the like of how he was punished, Allah helps him by granting him permission therein and does not prevent him from reciprocal treatment. This is because Allah is All-Clement (عفو) and All-Forgiving (غفور); He erases what this treatment and seeking revenge for an injury would otherwise entail of liability. It is as if punishment and inflicting dislike upon people is detestable in the system of life, except that Allah, the Glorified, erases the detestability within it and veils its evil effect when it is a punishment from an oppressed person against his oppressive transgressor in the like of how he was oppressed. Thus, He permits it for him and does not prevent him through prohibition or interdiction.

By this, the suitability of mentioning the attribute of forbearance (الحلم) at the end of the previous verse—"Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-forbearing"—also becomes apparent. It also becomes clear that the particle "then" (ثم) in His saying "and is then oppressed" denotes a sequence in mention (التراخي بحسب الذكر) rather than a sequence in time (التراخي بحسب الزمان).

As for what some have mentioned regarding the meaning of the verse: "Whoever penalizes the offender with the like of what he committed against him, and is then oppressed by the offender returning to punish him, Allah will surely help him against the one who oppressed him; indeed Allah is All-Clement and All-Forgiving toward the one who committed that punishment, as it was a departure from what is better (تركاً للأولى), for what is better is patience and forgiving the offender," as the Exalted said: **وَأَنْ تَعْفُوا أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوَى** "and to preclude is nearer to God-consciousness" (2:237), and He said: **فَمَنْ عَفَا وَأَصْلَحَ فَأَجْرُهُ عَلَى اللَّهِ** "but whoever excuses and settles the matter, his reward lies with Allah" (42:40), and He said: **وَلَمَنْ صَبَرَ وَغَفَرَ إِنَّ ذَلِكَ لَمِنْ عَزْمِ الأُمُورِ** "As for those who are patient and forgive, that is indeed among the most resolute of matters" (42:43).

To this, we respond: Firstly, when "then" (ثم) is taken to denote a sequence in time, it implies that the initial "punishment" was something other than "oppression" (البغي). General punishment is broader than being a crime (جناية), and its inclusion of both crime and non-crime would corrupt the meaning of the speech. Restricting it to mean a crime specifically—as they interpreted—is an interpretation for which there is no evidence from the wording itself.

Secondly, they interpreted "help" (النصرة) as ontological help (النصرة التكوينية) rather than legislative help (النصرة التشريعية). This would be an announcement that the Exalted helps the oppressed against the oppressor whenever he retaliates for the crime and is then oppressed again, yet reality sometimes deviates from this.

Thirdly, fighting the polytheists and struggling in the way of Allah are certainly among the instances of this verse. The implication of what they mentioned would be that abandoning such struggle by forgiving them is better than performing it, which is clearly false.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذَلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ يُولِجُ اللَّيْلَ فِي النَّهارِ وَيُولِجُ النَّهارَ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ بَصِيرٌ** "That is because Allah makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night, and because Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing" (22:61). The "passing" (إيلاج) of each of the night and day into the other is the arrival of one in the place of the other, like the arrival of the morning light upon the darkness of the night—as something that enters (يلج) into another—then its expansion and the day's occupation of the space that the night had occupied. Similarly, the arrival of the evening darkness upon the light of the day is like something entering into another, followed by its expansion and the encompassing of the night.

The referent of "That" (ذلك)—based on the previously mentioned meaning of help—is the triumph of the oppressed through his punishment of the oppressive transgressor. The meaning is that this help is because it is the sunnah (سنة) — *the established way or law* — of Allah to make one of two opposites or competing forces triumph over the other, just as He makes the night pass into the day and the day pass into the night. Furthermore, Allah is All-Hearing (سميع) of their words and All-Seeing (بصير) of their deeds; thus He helps the oppressed whose right is being consumed before His very eyes, and whose state of being asks Him, which He hears.

Other views have been mentioned regarding the meaning of the verse that do not align with the context, and we have deemed it better to overlook mentioning them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذَلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ وَأَنَّ مَا يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ هُوَ الْباطِلُ وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْكَبِيرُ** "That is because Allah is the Truth, and because what they invoke besides Him is falsehood, and because Allah is the All-exalted, the All-great" (22:62). The "That" refers to the help, or to it and the cause mentioned for it.

The restriction (الحصر) in His saying "because Allah is the Truth" and His saying "and because what they invoke besides Him is falsehood" is either in the sense that the Exalted is Truth (حق) unmixed with any falsehood, and that what they invoke besides Him—namely the idols—is falsehood (باطل) unmixed with any truth; thus He is capable of acting within the creation of things and ruling for or against them as He wills.

Or it is in the sense that the Exalted is Truth in the true reality of the word's meaning, being independent therein, such that there is no truth other than Him except what He has made true. And what they invoke besides Him—namely the idols, or rather everything that is relied upon and invoked for a need besides Allah—is the Falsehood, and nothing else; for the referent of "other than it" is Allah, the Glorified—so understand this. It is only falsehood because it possesses no truth through its own independence.

The meaning—on either interpretation—is that this acting within creation (التكوين) and legislation (التشريع) by Allah, the Glorified, is because He is the Truth through whose will every other truth is realized, and that their deities besides Allah and everything that an oppressive transgressor relies upon besides Him is falsehood, incapable of anything.

His saying: "and because Allah is the All-exalted, the All-great"—His Exaltation (علوه) is such that He is above all and nothing is above Him, and His Greatness (كبره) is such that He is never diminished by anything through humiliation or lowliness. These are branches of His being the Truth (الحق); that is, the Firm (الثابت) who is never subject to cessation, and the Existent (الموجود) who is never touched by non-existence.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ مَاءً فَتُصْبِحُ الأَرْضُ مُخْضَرَّةً إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَطِيفٌ خَبِيرٌ** "Have you not regarded that Allah sends down water from the sky, and then the earth turns green? Indeed Allah is all-subtle, all-aware" (22:63). This is a citation of evidence for the universality of power (القدرة) referred to earlier, through the sending down of water from the sky—meaning the direction of height—and the earth becoming green thereby.

His saying: "Indeed Allah is all-subtle, all-aware" is the reason for making the earth green by sending down water from the sky. The result of this reasoning and that citation of evidence is as if it were said: Allah sends down such-and-forth, and so it becomes such-and-forth, because He is All-Subtle (لطيف) and All-Aware (خبير), and this testifies to the universality of His power.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الأَرْضِ وَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُوَ الْغَنِيُّ الْحَمِيدُ** "To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, and Allah is indeed the All-sufficient, the All-laudable" (22:64). Its apparent meaning is that it is a predicate after a predicate for "Indeed" (إنّ), thus it is a completion of the reasoning in the previous verse. It is as if it were said: Indeed Allah is All-Subtle, All-Aware, the Owner of what is in the heavens and what is in the earth, acting within His kingdom as He wills with subtlety and awareness. It is also possible that it is a new sentence (استئناف) providing a reason based on His independence.

His saying: "and Allah is indeed the All-sufficient, the All-laudable" signifies His lack of need for any of His actions, as He is the All-Sufficient (الغني) in an absolute sense. Yet, these actions are beautiful and beneficial, for which He is praised, as He is the All-Laudable (الحميد) in an absolute sense. The combined implication of the two names is that the Exalted does nothing except that which is beneficial, but its benefit does not return to Him, but rather to the creation themselves.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمْ مَا فِي الأَرْضِ** "Have you not regarded that Allah has disposed for you whatever is in the earth..." (22:65) etc. This is another citation of evidence for the universality of power. The contrast between the disposal of what is in the earth and the disposal of the ships in the sea supports the view that "the earth" (الأرض) here refers to the land as opposed to the sea. Accordingly, following these two phrases with His saying: "and He holds the sky..." etc., gives the summary meaning that Allah has disposed for you what is in the heaven and the earth, its land and its sea.

"The sky" (السماء) refers to the direction of height and what is within it; thus Allah holds it from falling upon the earth except by His permission, such as falling meteorites, thunderbolts, and the like.

He concluded the verse with the attributes of affection (الرأفة) and mercy (الرحمة) to complete the blessing and as a favor upon the people.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَحْيَاكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ إِنَّ الإِنْسَانَ لَكَفُورٌ** "It is He who gave you life, then He makes you die, then He will give you life [again]. Indeed man is very ungrateful" (22:66). The use of the past tense in "gave you life" (أحياكم) indicates that the intended meaning is the life of this world. The importance of the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and life after death* — in this context necessitates that the intended meaning of His saying "then He will give you life [again]" is the life of the Hereafter on the Day of Resurrection, rather than the life of the intermediate realm (البرزخ).

This life, then death, then life, are among the greatest divine blessings with which He concluded the mention of favors; therefore, He followed it by saying: "Indeed man is very ungrateful."

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Jami‘ al-Jawami‘*: regarding His saying: "As for those who migrate..." until His saying "...all-knowing, all-forbearing"—it is narrated that they said: "O Messenger of Allah, as for those who were killed, we have known what Allah has given them of good. But we struggle with you just as they struggled; what is there for us if we die with you?" So Allah sent down these two verses.

In *al-Majma‘*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: "and whoever retaliates with the like of what he was made to suffer..."—it is narrated that the verse was revealed concerning a group of the polytheists of Mecca who met a group of Muslims when two nights remained of the month of Muharram. They said: "The companions of Muhammad do not fight in this month," so they attacked them. The Muslims implored them not to fight them in the sacred month, but they refused. So Allah gave the Muslims victory over them.

**I say:** It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Abi Hatim from Muqatil. However, the signs of weakness are apparent upon this report, for the polytheists used to hold the sacred months as forbidden. We have previously discussed His saying, the Exalted: **يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الشَّهْرِ الْحَرَامِ قِتَالٍ فِيهِ قُلْ قِتَالٌ فِيهِ كَبِيرٌ** "They ask you concerning warfare in the holy month. Say, ‘Warfare in it is a great [sin]...’" (2:217) in the second volume of this book, citing narrations regarding the story of ‘Abdullah ibn Jahsh and his companions that further increase the weakness of this report.
